posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
After visiting Pichon Comtesse, we bought sandwiches at the Boulangerie Bellet on the quayside front in Pauillac, as in the past, and headed back to the port at Saint Julien, for lunch by the side of the river. This is a good bet if the weather’s nice, since there’s normally nobody around and there are picnic tables with benches. The view is always terrific:

IMG_47841920×1440 149 KB

We then went to Grand Puy Lacoste. This time, I sent the same type of email as the second one to Pichon Comtesse, explaining that we were fans of GPL, etc and had an appointment confirmed with Emeline Borie, the eldest daughter of owner François-Xavier Borie. GPL do not have a wine tourism operation in place, with hostesses, etc, and I believe all visits are conducted by Emeline, so to be able to visit depends very much on her own availability.

This is the front view of the château, featuring our eldest, Emma, who had decided to photo-bomb my photo! The flag on the right is that of Aquitaine.

IMG_3928.HEIC4032×3024 2.82 MB

We began with a short history, walking around the buildings so Emeline could show us some of the vineyard. GPL is a lot further from the river than Pichon Comtesse, situated on quite hilly land - you can get some idea here - this is a slope facing north towards Pontet-Canet, which you can see on the hill in the background (I think she said it is mostly planted with Merlot):

IMG_3937.HEIC4032×3024 2.86 MB

Then we went inside the courtyard at the back of the château, past the family’s bedrooms. As Emeline made clear, this is still very much a family château, where they live most of the year. We bumped into François-Xavier on the way, who was carrying a hosepipe and trying to find the gardener to water his roses.

The cuvier has stainless steel tanks (no experiments with amphora here):

IMG_3931.HEIC4032×3024 2.74 MB

The cellar is temperature-controlled:

IMG_3935.HEIC4032×3024 2.27 MB

But although everything is modern, there’s something endearingly higgledy-piggledy about it all. For example, Emeline told us that when their oenologist, Christel Spinner, took up her post (I think in 2012), she asked for some new destemming machines, which F-X had never heard of - but he thought he had better get one to keep her happy. The machine was such a success that they now have three.

After the cellar, we stopped off to see the family chapel. It is still consecrated and in use. Emeline was quite happy for me to take photos but I didn’t feel it was right - I did however take one of the stainless glass window:

IMG_3934.HEIC3024×4032 2.13 MB

Then she decided to show us the family cellar:

IMG_3932.HEIC3024×4032 2.68 MB

This is not like the vinothèques you see in other châteaux, which are mainly for show, nor the large reserves of old vintages elsewhere which are kept back for future library releases via négociants, but the private collection of the Borie family. There are only bottles of various sizes of GPL, the oldest being 1943 (which you can see in the centre alcove along with 1945). The bottles are not labelled because of the humidity.

After that, we went to the tasting room which overlooks the vines:

IMG_3936.HEIC3024×4032 1.81 MB

This is a new idea - you can buy a case of GPL from one vintage, consisting of one double magnum, two magnums and four bottles. As Emeline explained, it’s really for parents to buy for a child, or a godchild (or similar), so that the lucky recipient can enjoy their birth-year wine over a lifetime:

IMG_3939.HEIC4032×3024 2 MB

This is a sample of the pebbles in the vineyard:

IMG_3940.HEIC3024×4032 1.46 MB

Emeline told us, chortling, about a well-known wine critic who mistook it for a spittoon - she can still recall the sight of the rather large gulp of wine oozing its way down through the pebbles!

We tasted the two 2014s, the Lacoste Borie and the GPL. The former was perfectly decent but nothing special, fine for early drinking, the latter was just as I expected - elegant, fresh and graceful, not overly tannic, nor too ripe or concentrated, a classic GPL which I’m glad I’ve got in my cellar.

Before we left, Emeline very kindly gave us all some little souvenir presents (we had seen the beehives in the garden):

IMG_3974.HEIC3024×4032 1.16 MB

GPL is definitely not a faceless corporate conglomerate where the château is only opened for company seminars and VIP visits, but a family-run business where they still have the human touch. As Emeline explained, the chances of it remaining as such in the future are probably slim - because of the prices paid by investors elsewhere and the inevitable knock-on effect on the value of their own property (and subsequent death duties). She reminisced about the families of former owners of other châteaux, with whom the Borie children would play every summer - I think Basile Tesseron’s recent departure came as quite a blow. Of course we’re not talking about a struggling vigneron in Entre-Deux-Mers, and this is not a family of artisans, nevertheless it was sad to hear, giving us a fin-de-siècle feeling as we left, conscious that once more, this was something we might not see again.

It was a wonderful experience. We were treated like guests of the family and it was definitely one of the most enjoyable visits we have done. Emeline Borie was exceptionally kind and very amusing company - we were sad to leave!

As at Pichon Comtesse, the visit was entirely free.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I guess the critic mistaking the glass of rocks for a dump bucket is better than someone thinking it was something to lick to taste the ‘mineralite’ !!!

Great writeup. GPL is such a stalwart.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: You weren’t pursued by the angry geese?

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: Another fabulous report - thank you! I love that boxed set concept.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: No, Emeline told us they are trained to only attack tax inspectors (and presumably TWA critics!)

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
That’s impressive … the turkeys that inhabit our neighborhoods attack anything shiny, like car chrome.

Kudos to GPL for not just taming the tannins, but convincing geese to work for the greater good.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
10000041391920×2672 342 KB

There are scant mentions of GPL’s deuxieme on WB, other than Julian’s trip report, and given how informative his photos + background colar are, I felt this tasting note might slide in here. The 2010 Lacoste Borie [Pauillac] could be better than the (untasted to me) 2014 L-B mentioned up thread. I have been drinking GPL since my early 20’s, over many vintages, albeit less so among recent (pricier!) releases … yet the 2010 is only year I’ve had their second, so a relative comparison between the grand vin, and other years, is harder to make. This 13.5% abv medium bodied red feels cabernet driven showing a currant, pencil, fennel bouquet. For me the palate is red berry sweetish, with low acid, and resolved tannin. I’d hoped to serve this with a leg of lamb that I had cubed up, marinated, skewered and finished under the broiler…but the grip is too loose to work with that. That is surprising for a ‘big year’ Pauillac, but it’s still delicious in a feminine way. I had initially thrown this into a gigantic Riedel 001 (probably 800ml) which is like drinking out of a decanter - and tends to save the decanting/aeration step - but if anything I like smaller, less breakable glasses when puttering about the kitchen.

Overall I’d give this a B+ but as others mention, the ‘value’ in seconds from well regarded estates tends to be tilted against the consumer nowadays.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thanks for posting, Arv - you confirm my own impressions over the years. Lacoste Borie is a decent wine but not a noteworthy second wine, unlike some of the other Pauillacs.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: love the idea of using Julian’s wonderful threads about his Bordeaux visits as places to put future tasting notes on wines from winery. Great idea Arv.

posts.name: Klaus S_T_0_T_2_E_1
posts.cooked: I had the good luck of a GPL visit and Julian is spot on, great visit, Emeline is a special host.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Glad you enjoyed it - and thanks for reminding me of what a great time we had! It was one of the more memorable visits we made.
